title: Investor demand, firm investment, and capital misallocation
abstract: Fluctuations in investor demand significantly affect firms’ valuation and access to capital. To quantify their real effects, we develop a dynamic investment model, incorporating both the demand and supply sides of capital. Strong investor demand relaxes financial constraints and facilitates equity issuance and investment, while weak demand encourages opportunistic share repurchases, crowding out investment. We estimate the model using indirect inference, matching the endogenous relationship between investor demand and firm policies. Our estimation reveals that demand fluctuations are important drivers of firm-level investment and economy-wide capital misallocation, accounting for 26.9% of dispersion in MPK and 23.4% of productivity losses.

article.1. Introduction: The history of the U.S. stock market is marked by striking booms and busts. During the Nasdaq bubble of the 1990s, for example, internet stocks traded at price levels that defied most rational pricing explanations. In the more recent “meme stocks” episode of 2021, prices of GameStop and many other stocks skyrocketed, opening up financing opportunities that would have otherwise been unavailable to these firms. These dramatic valuation episodes are often attributed to demand from investors who favor those stocks despite the mismatch between valuations and fundamentals. How does this type of demand affect the allocation of capital across firms? Does it promote investment in firms that are more productive and have greater financial needs? If not, what is the magnitude of the distortion in capital allocation resulting from investor demand? These questions speak directly to the central role of financial markets for efficient capital allocation.In this paper, we tackle these questions by developing and estimating a dynamic model in which firms make investment and financing decisions while facing capital provision driven by investor demand for their equity. The distinctive feature of our setting, compared to standard investment models, is the direct incorporation of time-varying demand from investors, which we discipline using detailed data on investor portfolios. Our model embeds the endogenous relationship between investor demand and firms’ optimal investment and financing decisions. Using the estimated model, we uncover significant responses of firms’ financial policies to investor demand. We also examine investment policies, enabling us to evaluate the impact of investor demand on the efficiency of capital allocation in U.S. public firms.Our demand-side model builds on the framework introduced by Koijen and Yogo (2019), which models investor demand using firm characteristics and fluctuations in a latent component in excess of firm fundamentals. While their study addresses asset pricing implications, our focus is on the firm side. We document that these demand-driven effects also have important implications for aggregate productivity and the efficiency of allocation of capital in the economy. Eliminating excess demand alleviates the misallocation of capital across firms, reducing the dispersion in the marginal product of capital by 26.9% and decreasing productivity losses by 23.4%.The focus of our model is on the interplay between firms and investors. Firms make financing and investment decisions after considering the equilibrium prices shaped by investor demand, and these decisions, in turn, feed back into investors’ demand functions. The direct effect of investor demand lies in its influence on firms’ financing decisions. High demand incentivizes firms to issue more shares, while weak demand induces firms to strategically buy back shares at favorable prices. Firms also adjust their leverage and cash decisions, all of which subsequently influence firm value, operational decisions, and capital accumulation. Because our characteristic-based demand system depends on firm attributes, this coordinated evolution of firm characteristics further affects investors’ demand for the firm’s stock. As such, our approach integrates the endogenous supply of capital from investors into a standard neoclassical investment model.Our model embeds two key channels through which investor demand impacts firms’ real investment decisions. First, fluctuations in investor demand create opportunities for market timing, enabling firms to capitalize on financial transactions. This additional source of profit alleviates financial constraints for firms and helps their investments converge to an unconstrained level (the constraint-easing effect). Second, firms’ engagement in financial transactions can have heterogeneous effects on their investment capacity, depending on realized excess demand (the dispersion effect). Positive excess demand encourages firms to issue equity, providing them with additional funds to invest. Negative excess demand prompts firms to reduce investments and prioritize profitable financial transactions in the equity market over investment efficiency. Contrary to the former effect, the dispersion effect can introduce an additional wedge in the cross section of the marginal product of capital, potentially harming capital allocation efficiency in the economy.To quantify these effects of investor demand, we estimate the parameters of our dynamic model using the simulated method of moments (SMM). The model parameters are divided into two groups: one describing investors’ demand system and the other governing firm dynamics. When estimating the demand parameters, it is crucial to consider the feedback effects of firm decisions. Therefore, we identify the key parameters of the demand system through an indirect inference approach, employing an auxiliary model that approximates the endogenous relationship between firm characteristics and latent excess demand, without resorting to the instrumental variables in Koijen and Yogo (2019). This indirect inference approach yields a set of coefficients that dictate investor demand based on observable firm characteristics, as well as variations in residual demand in excess of these observed features. Turning to firm dynamics, we match model-generated moments to real-data moments, identifying the set of parameters that shape firm policies and dynamics. Our estimated model parameters generate simulated data that closely match the key features of the actual data. Furthermore, we demonstrate our model’s external validity by showing its ability to replicate data features not directly targeted in the estimation, including firms’ overvaluation during seasoned equity offerings (SEOs).Using our estimated model, we conduct analyses to evaluate how firms respond to excess investor demand. First, we examine the flow of funds within firms and find that they allocate a substantial portion of their resources to timing excess investor demand, regardless of their financial constraints. However, this behavior crowds out investments, primarily in financially constrained firms, while less constrained firms use the proceeds from financial transactions largely to distribute dividends to existing shareholders. Second, we perform counterfactual analyses to assess the impact of sudden shifts in investor demand, resembling a surge in “meme” stock investors. We find that firms increase their equity issuance by more than tenfold, which leads to higher capital investments, though these projects tend to have low marginal returns. Overall, our findings highlight that excess investor demand can drive significant variation in firms’ real actions in the cross-section.We further examine how these firm-level effects of investor demand aggregate in the economy by quantifying the magnitude of demand-driven capital misallocation. Our analyses compare the allocation of physical capital under our baseline model with that under a counterfactual scenario where excess demand from investors is absent. Capital misallocation is measured using the variance of the marginal product of capital (MPK) in the cross section of firms and the average loss in the total factor productivity (TFP) in the economy, both of which capture deviations from efficient capital allocation across firms (Hsieh and Klenow, 2009). We find that excess investor demand results in significant increases in capital misallocation among firms, as the dispersion effect of investor demand outweighs the constraint-easing effect. In the counterfactual economy where excess investor demand is turned off, we observe a 26.9% reduction in the dispersion of MPK and a 23.4% decrease in TFP loss. These results suggest that fluctuations in investor demand can hinder the efficient allocation of capital in the real economy, as firms’ tendency to engage in financial market transactions creates conflicting incentives for the allocation of capital resources for investment efficiency.Our estimation provides additional interesting results. First, we compare our baseline model with an alternative model where we shut down the feedback loop between investor demand and firm policies. The baseline model aligns more closely with the data, matching the observed correlation between firms’ equity issuance and other characteristics. Second, fluctuations in investor demand are an important driver of misallocation, compared to other sources of misallocation established in previous studies (Midrigan and Xu, 2014; Moll, 2014; David et al., 2022). In particular, the impact of investor demand is more substantial than that arising from debt market and capital adjustment frictions, and it is comparable in magnitude to the risk considerations, which is a key driver of measured capital misallocation among US firms (David et al., 2022).Finally, we examine the sources of fluctuations in latent excess demand, which Koijen and Yogo (2019) attribute to investor sentiment—excess demand is unobservable to econometricians and not linked to firm-specific characteristics. Our findings show that model-implied latent demand correlates more strongly with non-fundamental asset pricing factors than with fundamental ones and is positively associated with sentiment indicators commonly used in the literature, such as those based on news releases, order imbalances, and the tone of earnings conference calls. This finding supports the sentiment-driven interpretation. Additionally, subsample estimations reveal that firms with higher household ownership and greater sentiment volatility face larger fluctuations in latent demand, leading to greater distortions in capital allocation.Our paper contributes to several strands of literature. First, it adds to the growing literature on the allocation of capital in the real and financial markets. Following the seminal work of Hsieh and Klenow (2009) which documents substantial gaps in resource misallocation across firms in China and India, prior studies have quantitatively evaluated various real and financial frictions that can contribute to such misallocation, including debt constraints (Midrigan and Xu, 2014, Bai et al., 2018), capital adjustment costs, technology dispersion, and information frictions (David and Venkateswaran, 2019), and taxes (Glover and Levine, 2021). We contribute to this literature by focusing on a new source of capital misallocation driven by investor demand. Using a tightly disciplined dynamic corporate finance model, we study this demand-driven channel and document its large quantitative effect on capital allocation efficiency. Our modeling framework is closely related to those used in several recent studies that quantify sources of misallocation in the economy. David et al. (2022) develop a dynamic model, linking misallocation to firms’ exposure to macroeconomic risk. Relatedly, Kilic and Tuzel (2023) demonstrate that firms’ exposure to growth spurts also helps explain significant variations in their MPK. Zhao and Whited (2021) estimate the real losses arising from the cross-sectional misallocation of financial liabilities, revealing a much higher distortionary cost among Chinese firms than among U.S. firms.Table 1. Our paper belongs to the growing body of research that quantifies the real consequence of such equity misvaluation. Warusawitharana and Whited (2016) study a dynamic investment model to estimate the extent to which mispricing, reflected in firms’ market-to-book ratios, drives investment and financing. Dessaint et al. (2019) document that firms adjust their investment to stock market noise induced by mutual fund flows. Binsbergen and Opp (2019) investigate the implications of cross-sectional asset pricing anomalies for asset allocation. Our study is distinct in that we directly model the interaction between firms and the demand of equity investors. We use detailed institutional holdings data combined with a demand estimation strategy that is flexible to match features in the data. Our estimation yields realistic demand elasticity and variations of demand across investors and time, making it well-suited for quantitatively analyzing misallocation effects.Third, our paper also contributes to the growing body of literature on the demand-based approach in asset pricing, à la Koijen and Yogo (2019). Existing papers in the literature focus primarily on the asset pricing implications of investor demand, treating firms’ financing and investment decisions as exogenous. We contribute to this literature by embedding investors’ demand into a classical dynamic investment model of heterogeneous firms with rich real and financial frictions. This approach allows us to endogenize the evolution of firm characteristics and the supply of firm equity. We jointly estimate the supply and demand side of the model using firm balance sheet information and detailed investor holdings data.Methodologically, our paper belongs to the growing literature that employs structural model calibration or estimation to address corporate finance questions in capital investment, leverage, and valuation (e.g., Hennessy and Whited, 2005; Rampini and Viswanathan, 2010; Korteweg, 2010; Morellec et al., 2012; Glover and Levine, 2017; Corhay et al., 2020; Terry, 2023). We contribute to the literature by developing a framework that can be applied to explore a wide range of questions pertaining to the real effects of investor demand and its macroeconomic implications.
article.2. Motivating empirical facts: Using annual data from CRSP and Compustat, we begin by summarizing several stylized facts regarding firms’ investment behavior, equity issuance, share repurchases, and stock market valuation. The sample period spans from 1980 to 2021, with 1980 serving as the starting point to align with the availability of institutional holdings data.1First, equity issuance is less frequent than debt issuance; however, when it does occur, it serves as a more substantial source of financing. In our sample period, the probability of a firm issuing equity in a given year is 14.4%, compared to 42.4% for debt issuance.2 Conditional on equity issuance, the average amount raised is 18.8% of firms’ assets, with issuances at the 75th percentile reaching as high as 23.9%. In contrast, conditional on positive debt issuance, the average and 75th percentile figures are lower, at 12.4% and 15.2%, respectively. Therefore, while equity issuance is less common, it can still serve as a substantial source of financing. In contrast, share repurchases are more frequent, occurring 48.6% of the time, and they tend to follow a smoother pattern. When firms repurchase shares, they typically buy back an average of 3.6% of their assets. We summarize these key statistics of equity issuance, debt issuance, and repurchases in Table G.6 in the Internet Appendices.Second, firms are more likely to issue equity when their valuation is high, as previously documented in the market timing literature (e.g., Baker and Wurgler, 2002, Baker et al., 2003). In Panel A of Table 2, we confirm this empirical relationship by regressing firms’ equity issuance on several proxies for mispricing, including Tobin’s Q, discretionary accruals3 , and stock price changes. The results remain significant after we control for firm characteristics and the firm and year fixed effects.Third, the proceeds from equity issuance are positively associated with firms’ investments. Panel B of Table 2 presents results based on the regressions of firms’ investment on equity issuance, showing a significant positive relationship across all specifications. Quantitatively, column (2) indicates that a one percent increase in equity issuance is associated with a 15.4 basis point rise in investment. By comparison, a one percent increase in debt issuance is associated with a comparable increase of 25.7 basis points in investment. These findings are consistent with prior literature. Baker et al. (2003) demonstrate that firms’ investment decisions can be quite sensitive to non-fundamental moves in stock prices through the equity financing channel. Farhi and Panageas (2004) and Gilchrist et al. (2005) provide evidence that investment both at the firm and aggregate level is responsive to mispricing indicators. Leveraging the bubble in Japanese equity markets from 1987 to 1989, Chirinko and Schaller (2001) observe that it led to a 6%–9% increase in business fixed investment.Table 2. The sample period is 1980–2021. We exclude firms in the utility (SIC 4900–4999) and financial (SIC 6000–6999) sectors. The balance-sheet variables are obtained from Compustat, stock prices are obtained from CSRP, and portfolio weights are calculated using the Form 13F data. All variables are defined in Table 1.Importantly, the real effect of equity issuance varies significantly across firms, depending on their reliance on equity financing. We construct three indices to proxy for firms’ ex-ante equity dependence. The first is the KZ-index (Kaplan and Zingales, 1997), originally used in Baker et al. (2003) as a measure of equity dependence. The second measure is the financial flexibility index, as in Doidge et al. (2009). This index is calculated by awarding one point for each of the following conditions: the firm has high cash and liquid assets, it pays high dividends, and it maintains low capital expenditures.4 Firms with lower FF scores are considered less financially flexible and, therefore, more equity-dependent. The third is a more direct measure based on the firm’s historical financing patterns—the ED (equity minus debt) index, which is calculated as the difference between the average equity issuance-to-asset ratio and the debt issuance-to-asset ratio over the past rolling five-year window for each firm. We interact each of these indices with our main variable of interest, . Our results in columns (4) through (6) reveal that the positive link between investment and equity issuance is strengthened for firms that are more equity-dependent, as indicated by the coefficient estimates on the interaction terms. This cross-sectional variation reflects substantial economic magnitudes. According to the results in column (6), when a firm’s equity dependence, measured by its historical equity issuance, increases from the bottom to the top 5th percentile (these percentiles are reported in Table 3), the corresponding investment-issuance relationship increases by 0.048, an around 40% increase relative to the base value of 0.125. Using other equity dependence proxies also yields similar quantitative effects. Taken together, our results suggest that for average firms, the sensitivity of investment to equity issuance proceeds may be modest, but for firms that rely more on equity financing, the proceeds from equity are associated with a significant increase in investment. All results presented in Table 2 remain robust when we adopt an alternative specification that employs a dummy variable for equity issuance instead of the continuous equity issuance-to-asset ratio. The robustness results are reported in Tables G.9 and G.10 in the Internet Appendix.Recent anecdotes from the 2021 “meme stock” episode also provide an intriguing case in which sentiment-driven stock price surges lead to financing and investment opportunities that might not have otherwise been available. During 2021, six stocks frequently referred to as “meme stock” – GameStop, AMC, Bed Bath & Beyond, National Beverage, Koss, and Tesla – experienced, on average, a 73% increase in equity issuance and a 43% increase in investment.While our analysis in this section does not aim to establish causal relationships, it highlights the potentially important interactions between firms’ market timing incentives and their real decisions. Importantly, the relationship exhibits significant heterogeneity across firms, suggesting that the proceeds from equity issuance benefit some firms more than others, which could bear important implications for the capital allocation efficiency in the economy.
article.3. Model: In this section, we build a model that endogenizes the demand and supply sides of equity capital. In our model, firms face capital provision that arises from time-varying investor demand; in response, they make optimal decisions on share issuance and repurchase, dividend payout, leverage, and investment. These decisions, in turn, feed back into investors’ demand functions.3.1. (14) represents the percent change in the stock price when the firm issues one percent of new equity, capturing the price impact of net share issuance.Notably, there are two key differences between the price impacts discussed above and those in Koijen and Yogo (2019). First, in Koijen and Yogo (2019), firm characteristics are taken as given, whereas, in our framework, firms optimally choose their policies based on the realization of their excess demand. These policies, which represent the firm’s characteristics, in turn, feed back into the investors’ demand system. As a result, ∂xt(n)∂ϵt(n) shows up in the price impact equations.8 Second, while Koijen and Yogo (2019) assume a fixed supply of shares, we account for firms’ endogenous issuance decisions. When a firm issues non-zero percentages of stocks, variations in the market price also affect the dollar value the firm can receive from the issuance, which can trigger further changes in firm characteristics, affecting investors’ demand for the firm’s shares. These price impacts of excess demand and equity issuance, therefore, capture the interaction between investors and firms.3.4. The firm’s optimization problemAt the beginning of each period, firms observe the aggregate productivity of the economy, at, specified in Eq. (2), and Γt, which denotes the distribution of firms in the economy. This distribution is relevant for the firm’s decision-making because investors’ portfolio weights depend not only on the firm’s own characteristics but also on the joint distribution of characteristics across other firms in their portfolios, as outlined in Eq. (12). Therefore, Γt influences the price at which the firm can issue or repurchase shares through the investor demand channel discussed in Section 3.3.In addition to the aggregate productivity shocks, firms also face idiosyncratic productivity shocks, z, defined in Eq. (3), and idiosyncratic excess demand shocks, ϵ. We model the logarithm of ϵ as an AR(1) process: (15)log(ϵt+1)=ρϵlog(ϵt)+νt+1ϵ,withνt+1ϵ∼N(0,σϵ2).Firms then produce and simultaneously announce their current-period investment, debt, and dividend policies. They can also tap the capital market to issue or repurchase additional shares; the price at which these transactions take place is determined in equilibrium by both firms’ actions and investors’ demand. When the equity market transaction prices deviate from a firm’s fundamental value, it results in a transfer of wealth from investors who trade with the firm to those who do not. Firm managers design their market-timing strategies to maximize the value of their controlling shareholders who do not engage directly in equity transactions with the firm. This means that, when the firm issues equity, the managers will aim to maximize the value of the existing shareholders, while in the event of a share repurchase, the managers will maximize the value of the retaining shareholders. The setup closely follows that in Warusawitharana and Whited (2016), who provide detailed discussions on incorporating managers’ market-timing incentives into an otherwise standard neoclassical model of investment.The Bellman equation for the firm’s controlling shareholder can be written recursively as follows (for simplicity, we drop the time subscript and firm indexing): (16)V(K,C,z,ϵ;a,Γ)=max{Δs,E,D,I,C′}(1−τd)D+11+ΔsEz,ϵ,am(a,a′)V(K′,C′,z′,ϵ′;a′,Γ′), where K and C represent the existing level of physical capital and the cash balance, I represents investments, Δs and E are the fraction and dollar amount of equity issuance, respectively, D is the dividend payment, and τd denotes the dividend tax rate. We multiply the continuation value of the firm by 11+Δs to capture the dilution effect from new equity issuance.We denote m(⋅) as the stochastic discount factor (SDF) for the controlling shareholders.9 Following Zhang (2005) and David et al. This formulation allows us to capture, in a simple manner, a time-varying and countercyclical price of risk as observed in the data. Similar to Warusawitharana and Whited (2016), controlling shareholders value the firm using this SDF, which represents the marginal utility of their equity wealth. The equity wealth of the controlling holders is measured using the intrinsic value of equity. This is consistent with the notion that these controlling shareholders do not directly engage in any equity transactions with the firm. article.4. Estimation: In this section, we first outline our strategy for identifying the parameters that govern investor demand and firm optimization. We then present the estimation results and follow with a battery of validation tests to evaluate the reliability of both our model and the indirect inference approach.4.1. DataOur analysis uses data on stock prices from the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP) and accounting data from Compustat, which we describe in Section 2.To identify parameters that pertain to investor demand, we draw institutional stock holdings data from the Thompson Reuters Institutional Holdings Database, which is based on Form 13F filings. All US-based institutions with assets under management exceeding $100 million dollars are required to report their long-side holdings in the 13F filings if their holding in a publicly listed stock is greater than 10,000 shares or $200,000. The sample contains 173,238 firm-year observations and 17,130 distinct firms. We merge these institutional holdings data with the CRSP-Compustat data and filter out any holdings data that do not match with the CRSP-Compustat database. We compute portfolio weights for common stocks (i.e., CRSP share code 10 or 11) held by a 13F institution as the ratio of the dollar amount held to the total dollar amount of all stocks held by the institution. Stocks with missing characteristics or returns and those with CRSP share code 12 or 18 (foreign companies or real estate investment trusts) are classified as the outside asset (i.e., n=0), consistent with the methodology outlined in Koijen and Yogo (2019). Using available data, we construct the key variables used in our estimation. Table 3 presents summary statistics for our sample.4.2. IdentificationOur estimation starts by identifying parameters that pertain to investor demand, including β=βme,βbe,βprof,βinv,βdiv,βmktbeta, which captures how demand responds to log market equity, log book equity, investment, profitability, dividends, and market beta, respectively, and ρϵ,σϵ, which captures the persistence and standard deviation of latent excess demand that firms face.We estimate the demand-side parameters using an indirect inference approach, which involves first running the following regression in the data11: (21)logwi,t(n)∼γme⋅men,t+γbe⋅ben,t+γprof⋅profn,t+γinv⋅invn,t+γdiv⋅divn,t+γmktbeta⋅mktbetan,t+μi,t(n).If latent demand is exogenous, i.e., ϵi,t(n) in Eq. (10) is uncorrelated with the firm characteristics, such as market and book equity and investment, then the vector of coefficient estimates, γ̂, should be an unbiased estimate of the true structural demand parameters, β, allowing us to equate the estimated residuals in the regression, μi,t(n) to the log of latent excess demand. This condition is, however, likely to be violated because of inherent endogeneity in market equity and other supply-side policies.To tackle this challenge, we run the same regression of investor portfolio holdings w(n) on firm characteristics x(n), using the simulated data from the model. Note first that the estimated regression coefficients from the actual data, γ̂, in Eq. (21) can be viewed as the true parameters, β, plus a bias that arises from the feedback effect of demand shocks on supply-side policies. Thus, the regression coefficients can provide useful information on the underlying preference parameters and serve as effective identifying moments. Furthermore, our model endogenizes firms’ investment and financing policies based on their information set, including their knowledge of the latent demand, as specified in Eq. (16). Therefore, the regression coefficients obtained using our simulated data also capture similar endogenous feedback between latent demand and the observed firm characteristics that should be present in the data, leading to a consistent mapping between the model and data moments. By matching these regression coefficients, γ̂, which capture feedback from the supply-side effects, we can effectively back out the underlying structural demand parameters, β. Next, we identify the autocorrelation and standard deviation of latent demand that firms face, ρϵ,σϵ, by matching the autocorrelation and standard deviation of the residual in the auxiliary Eq. (21).This identification strategy for structural parameters β follows the indirect inference approach employed in a large body of literature, wherein the complexity of the setup rules out a direct inference approach to match the deep structural parameters (Gourieroux et al., 1993, Cooper and Haltiwanger, 2006). An alternative approach to indirect inference is to use instrumental variables (IVs) for firm characteristics (Koijen and Yogo, 2019). We later provide a more detailed discussion of our approach vis-à-vis the IV approach in Section 4.5.Our estimation of the firm-side parameters relies on the SMM, through which we seek to identify the set of parameter values that enables the simulated firm behaviors to track the actual data as closely as possible. The success of SMM estimation hinges critically on selecting moments that are sensitive to variations in underlying structural parameters, which we explain below.We use the autocorrelation and standard deviation of firms’ income-to-assets ratios to identify the law of motion for the idiosyncratic productivity process, ρz,σz. We include in our set of moments the mean of investment to determine the depreciation rate, δ, and the standard deviation of investment to determine the cost of capital adjustment, ξ. To identify the linear equity issuance cost parameter, ϕ1, we use the mean and standard deviation of firms’ percentage of equity issuance, while the fixed issuance cost parameter, ϕ0, is identified using the observed frequency of equity issuance in the data. We include the mean and standard deviation of firms’ share repurchases to ensure that we can also match the net changes in firms’ outstanding shares over time. Our estimation aims to match the levels and volatilities of the issuance and repurchase decisions as they reflect firms’ optimal reactions to investors’ excess demand, which is the key focus of our model. We further include firms’ average dividend payout in our set of moments as dividends and share repurchases are substitutable ways of redistributing cash, and matching firms’ tendency to pay dividends is important to ensure that our model can capture this substitution. We use firms’ average leverage to pin down the pledgeability parameter, θ. Lastly, we use firms’ average income-to-assets ratio and the market-to-book ratio to identify the fixed operating cost parameter, f, and the curvature of firms’ production function, α.In addition to the firm-level parameters, we also need to estimate the aggregate parameters that govern the stochastic discount factor, including {γ0,γ1,σζ}. These parameters are identified using aggregate stock market moments, such as the Sharp Ratio. Importantly, the aggregate moments predicted by the model are influenced by the firm-level parameters we discuss above, creating interdependence. To deal with the interdependence, we adopt an iterative approach. First, we fix the values of {γ0,γ1,σζ} and estimate the remaining firm-level parameters in the over-identified SMM system. Next, we hold the firm-level parameters at their estimated values and calibrate {γ0,γ1,σζ} to match the aggregate stock market moments. We iterate the above process to ensure that the parameters converge, accounting for the interdependence of these parameters in generating the firm-level and aggregate moments. Specifically, we calibrate γ0 and γ1 in Eq. (18) to match the average annual excess return of 7.69% and average Sharpe ratio of 0.174. The standard deviation of exposure to aggregate shocks, σζ, is calibrated to match the dispersion in expected stock returns, which is 0.221.124.3. (21). Our estimated βme suggests that investors exhibit fairly inelastic demand. In many traditional macro-finance models, investor demand curves are virtually flat, implying highly elastic demand. However, empirical elasticity estimates for individual stocks typically fall within the range of 0.3 to 1.6, as noted by Gabaix and Koijen (2021). Our model, which leverages granular investor portfolio data, is consistent with these elasticity estimates in the literature. The estimated inelastic demand implies that our model can generate sizable price impacts without requiring excessively volatile ϵ. Our estimated σϵ is 0.0931, comparable to the volatility of firms-level idiosyncratic productivity shocks. By building on the strengths of the demand estimation approach from Koijen and Yogo (2019), our model produces plausible estimates for investor demand characteristics, making it well-suited for quantifying the associated misallocation effects. Our estimation results also suggest that investors’ demand loads significantly positively on market equity, book equity, profitability, investment, and dividends, while showing a significant negative relationship with market beta.In Panel C, we report the firm-side parameters. For example, the curvature of the production function is close to two-thirds, physical capital depreciates at a rate of approximately 15% per year, and firms can collateralize about 80% of their assets. These estimates are largely consistent with those documented in the literature (see, e.g., Nikolov et al., 2021).Table 5 presents the moment conditions used in our SMM estimation. The first eight moment conditions involve the regression coefficients and the residuals from the auxiliary demand equation (21), while the remaining 13 moment conditions correspond to characteristics of firms’ balance sheet variables. We find that all the simulated moments stay reasonably close to their real data counterparts. The model’s close fit is not mechanical, as we have an over-identified system with more moment conditions than parameters.4.4. Model validationTo further validate our model, we proceed to investigate moments that we do not directly target in our estimation.First, we analyze firms’ empirical policy functions (Bazdresch et al., 2018), which demonstrate how firms’ optimal policies, specifically equity issuance and investment decisions, vary in response to changes in their underlying state variables, which we summarize by a quadruple, {K,C,z,ϵ}. Since a firm’s productivity shock, z, and its excess demand, ϵ, are unobservable, we use observable transformations of these unobservable state variables. In particular, we rely on the firm’s current-period income-to-assets ratio, driven by the realization of its TFP shock, and its beginning-of-period market-to-book ratio, which reflects how the market valuation varies relative to the size of book equity, potentially influenced by the investors’ demand. We regress a firm’s investment and equity issuance on these variables, along with its beginning-of-period leverage while controlling for firm size, the firm and year fixed effects. The coefficients thus reflect, on average, how changes in a firm’s state variables are associated with variations in its observed optimal decisions. Following Bazdresch et al. (2018), we first transform the raw variables into quartiles based on their ranking within a given year and then estimate the regression coefficients using these quartile variables as moments. The results of this regression are presented in column (1) of Table 6.We run the same regression using simulated data from the model, and the coefficients are reported in column (2). Notably, our model can closely match the empirical policy functions, both qualitatively and quantitatively. In particular, the regression coefficients reported in columns (1) and (2) have the same signs and are of comparable magnitudes. Using Monte Carlo simulations, Bazdresch et al. (2018) show that benchmarks based on firms’ policy functions are powerful in detecting model mis-specifications. The close match on the untargeted moments hence further validates that our model realistically captures key aspects in firms’ decision-making process.To further highlight the significance of the investor-firm interface, we compare the predictions of the baseline model with those of a counterfactual scenario where the impact of investors’ excess demand on firm policies is removed by setting the mean and standard deviation of log(ϵ) to zero. The regression coefficients under this counterfactual scenario are presented in column (3). The results indicate a significant deterioration in the overall fit of the policy-based moments. Specifically, the counterfactual model fails to match the relationship between the firm’s equity issuance and both its market-to-book ratio and leverage. Moreover, the counterfactual model performs considerably worse in matching firms’ investment responses to the transformed state variables.Second, we directly examine the connection between investors’ excess demand and firms’ decisions to issue shares. Empirical investigation of this connection requires constructing a measure for investors’ excess demand, which is unobservable. Therefore, we rely on a model-implied measure. In particular, we infer the implied excess demand for each investor-firm-quarter such that the model-predicted portfolio weight (specified in Eq. (12)) based on the observed firm characteristics and our demand estimates from Table 4, matches the actual portfolio weight observed for each stock in the data.14Our findings indicate a strong positive correlation between excess demand and firms’ equity issuance decisions—an interquartile change in ϵ is associated with 1.46% increase in equity issuance in the model, compared with 1.12% in the data. In addition, the increased equity issuance does translate into higher investments, as we show in Section 2. Based on the results in column (1), Panel B of Table 2, a one-percentage-point increase in equity issuance is associated with a 20.4-basis-point change investment rate. Our model simulation predicts a change of 23.7 basis points, closely matching the magnitude.Finally, as a further external validity test of the model, we perform a separate back-of-the-envelope check by comparing the model-implied misvaluation at seasoned equity offerings (SEOs) with the empirical evidence documented in the literature. Loughran and Ritter (2000) note that firms undertaking SEOs experience a three-year abnormal return of −22%. By tracking the five-year stock performance post-issuance, Loughran and Ritter (1995) conclude that SEO firms issue at an average overvaluation of 30%. In our model simulation, firms that issue equity face an average mispricing, as defined in Eq. (19), of 33.85%. The magnitude aligns closely with the findings from prior studies. The success of our model in capturing realistic mispricing can be attributed to its inelastic investor demand estimates, which can generate sizeable price impacts without the need for extreme swings in excess demand. As reported in Table 5, our model also closely matches the share of new issuance and stock repurchases. Taken together, these results suggest that our model can generate realistic profit opportunities from firms’ market timing.4.5. Comparison with alternative estimation strategiesIn this section, we discuss the implications of our estimated investor demand parameters and compare them with those based on alternative approaches. We begin by showing that the investor-firm feedback mechanism is operating in our model. Specifically, we compare the deep structural parameter estimates for the investor demand system, β,ρϵ,σϵ, with the estimates from the auxiliary Eq. (21).Columns (1) and (2) of Table 7 present these results. In principle, if latent demand is orthogonal to firm characteristics, the parameter estimates in these two columns should be equal. However, Fig. 2 demonstrates that firms’ optimal policies in a given period depend on latent excess demand. This endogeneity, arising from the interaction between investor demand and firm policies, creates a wedge between the structural parameters and the regression coefficients from the auxiliary equation.We also compare our results with estimates obtained using an alternative instrumental variable (IV) approach, as described in Koijen and Yogo (2019). Their approach addresses the endogenous determination of market-clearing prices by exploiting variation in the investment universe and assets under management across investors. Following Koijen and Yogo (2019), we first construct the instrument variable for market equity for each firm using the actual data, assuming that all investors hold equal-weighted portfolios in their investment universes. We then estimate the demand parameters, β, using the counterfactual market equity, which is orthogonal to the realized shocks to investor demand, as an instrument for the firms’ market-clearing equity valuation.Download: Download high-res image (111KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. For the firm value and policies, we calculate the average across all state variables (except ϵ) using the ergodic distribution.Koijen and Yogo’s (2019) IV approach and our indirect inference method both aim to address the endogeneity of latent demand and market prices. Koijen and Yogo (2019) correct the bias arising from endogeneity by isolating changes along the demand curve to estimate the slope of investor demand. Our approach, however, incorporates firms’ endogenous supply of shares into the model, resulting in the endogenous determination of market-clearing prices through the interaction of supply and demand forces in both the simulated data from the model and the actual data. This ensures that the mapping between the simulated and actual data is consistent, allowing us to back out the underlying structural demand parameters.Table 7 demonstrates that, despite the methodological differences between our approach and that of Koijen and Yogo (2019), both methods correct the bias in the same direction. The estimates of βme from both approaches (columns 2 and 3) are substantially lower than the OLS coefficient estimate obtained directly from the auxiliary equation (column 1). Additionally, both approaches yield larger βbe compared to the estimate from the auxiliary equation.Despite these similarities, there are important differences between our approach and that of Koijen and Yogo (2019). On the one hand, our method accounts for the endogenous determination of market equity and other firm characteristics. As illustrated in Fig. 2, investor demand influences firms’ policies, creating further wedges between the regression coefficients and the structural demand parameters. On the other hand, our model abstracts from certain firm-level heterogeneity in the data. In Internet Appendix C.2, we discuss ways to address unobserved heterogeneity when constructing moments.van der Beck (2022) proposes an alternative method for identifying investors’ demand elasticity by analyzing the time-series relationship between changes in investor portfolio holdings (flows) and stock price fluctuations. How would adopting this approach affect our model’s predictions quantitatively? To address this question, we first assume that the true data-generating process for investors’ portfolio holdings follows Eq. (12). Based on this assumption, we regress model-simulated flows on stock price changes, yielding a coefficient of 0.2871. This estimate closely aligns with the demand elasticity in van der Beck (2022) (Figure 3, without IV). Next, we re-estimate our model using the demand moments from the flow-based estimates, with the results reported in Table E.2 of the Internet Appendix. Notably, this alternative approach produces demand and firm-level parameter estimates that are closely aligned with the baseline. Using the new estimates, we also verify that our model can recover a holdings-level based γme of 0.7433, which is almost identical to our baseline estimate.In reality, investor demand can exhibit greater complexity than what is captured by the data-generating process we consider. Hence, a crucial question is how different estimation strategies would lead to varying conclusions when the true data-generating process is unknown. A side-by-side comparison of demand coefficients under alternative strategies in Table 7 reveals two key observations. First, all three methods consistently produce significantly lower coefficient on market equity and, as a result, higher price elasticities compared to the plain OLS approach, which does not account for endogenous pricing. Second, our estimated structural demand coefficient on market equity (0.34) falls between the estimates in Koijen and Yogo (2019) (0.65) and van der Beck (2022) (0.3–0.35), and aligns more closely with the latter. Third, our approach yields significantly lower coefficients on dividends and investment, which are direct choices of firms in our model. article.5. Investor demand and firm policies: In this section, we quantify the impact of investor demand on firm policies. To this end, we begin by illustrating the model mechanisms. Then, using the parameter estimates from our model, we investigate how fluctuations in latent demand can influence the sources and uses of funds across firms. Finally, we consider changes in a firm’s investor composition, with a particular focus on “meme” stock investors, assessing how these shifts could affect firms’ optimal policies and their return on investments.5.1. Firm policies and valuationsWe first verify, in Fig. 1, that there exists a monotonic relationship between investor demand and the price impact on a firm’s stock. The price impact represents the premium/discount in market prices over intrinsic values that arises from investors’ demand and firms’ issuance decisions. Thus, stocks that experience positive excess demand shocks trade at higher prices than their fundamental values, facilitating firms to issue additional equity shares, while firms that face negative demand experience price discounts.We now analyze how price impacts from latent excess demand influence a firm’s intrinsic value, as defined in Bellman Eq. (16). Panel A of Fig. 2 shows that the intrinsic value follows a U-shape with excess demand, indicating that both large positive and negative demand benefit the firm. With significant negative demand, the firm can repurchase shares at favorable prices. However, as logϵ approaches zero, this advantage declines. As logϵ further increases, the firm is incentivized to issue shares instead. We formally derive this result in Internet Appendix A.4 and illustrate it graphically in Panel B of Fig. 2.Excess demand also affects a firm’s cash holdings. Panel C reveals a positive relationship between cash holdings and latent excess demand, which we formally derived in Internet Appendices A.4 and A.5 by examining the firm’s Lagrangian for optimal cash policy. Our analysis indicates that with positive excess demand, firms issue more equity to capitalize on favorable price impacts, easing financial constraints and lowering the marginal value of internal liquidity. Conversely, with negative excess demand, firms rely on internal cash to finance both their investments and repurchases, which tightens their financial constraints, leading to a higher marginal value of cash.Panel D shows a positive relationship between excess demand and firm investment, formally derived in Internet Appendix A.6 by analyzing the firm’s Lagrangian with respect to optimal investment. Our analysis suggests that negative excess demand could induce firms to cut investments and compromise investment efficiency to capitalize on profitable financial transactions in the equity market. As excess demand increases and becomes more positive, there is no longer competition for the use of funds between share repurchases and capital investments. Firms can benefit from more relaxed financial conditions by making more investments. Notably, change in investment can also feed back to investors’ demand, as investment is a characteristic in the investors’ demand system. Therefore, when firms choose their optimal investment, they need to take into account how it impacts investor demand for their stocks, both now and in the next period. This feedback loop between investor demand and firm investment is unique to our setting and is absent in more traditional dynamic investment models.5.2. Financial constraints and flows of fundsExpanding on the implications from Fig. 2, we now investigate how the effect of investors’ demand varies across firms with varying financial constraints, focusing on how these firms generate and utilize cash flows.Table 8 presents the average sources and uses of funds (as percentages of each firm’s total funds) for four subgroups of simulated firms, categorized by their latent demand (ϵ) and their distance to the collateral constraint (θ−−CK). Firms with a distance to collateral constraint below the median are labeled “Constrained” firms, while those above the median are classified as “Less Constrained”.Our results reveal several interesting findings. First, we confirm that low demand (low ϵ) incentivizes firms to repurchase shares while high ϵ encourages equity issuance for both the constrained and less constrained subsamples. Second, we find that the incentive to engage in financial transactions significantly decreases the proportion of funding allocated to investment among more financially constrained firms. Columns (1) and (2) show that low ϵ firms allocate only 32.71% of their funds to investments, while high ϵ firms allocate 39.68%. The results show a sharp contrast to the less financially constrained group—investment percentages show little change despite fluctuations in underlying investor demand. These less constrained firms mainly use the cash proceeds from equity market transactions on dividends and cash retention.5.3. The effects of changing investor clienteleIn this section, we further quantify the impact of investor demand on firm investment. In particular, we consider a scenario where firms see a surge in investor demand that is unrelated to the fundamentals of firms. This exercise is particularly relevant in light of the “meme stock” frenzy of 2021, which created profits and financing opportunities for these firms. To capture the effect of such investor demand, we first compute the average realized ϵ faced by the meme stocks from Eq. (10), using the actual investor holdings data in 2021 and the estimated demand parameters reported in Table 4.15 The average meme stock has a logϵmeme over 4 standard deviations above the sample mean, which is consistent with our prior that these stocks are held by overly optimistic investors with excessively high demand. Next, we randomly select a subsample of firms in our simulated economy and counterfactually change their investor clientele by replacing a fraction X of their existing investors with those who invest in meme stocks and have an ϵmeme. In this counterfactual analysis, we keep all other aspects of the firms unchanged, especially their productivity. This setting allows us to attribute any resulting variations in firm characteristics to changes in investor clientele. We explore a range of values for X from 10% to 90% and assess the impact of these changes in investor clientele on observed firm policies.The results of this exercise, reported in Table 9, suggest that, first, firms with a high presence of meme-stock investors experience elevated share prices, which can be over 80% higher compared with the baseline firms that experience no changes in investor clientele. Second, and intuitively, high stock prices incentivize these firms to issue substantially more shares. Third, the increased financial resources facilitate investments by those firms—firms with 90% meme stock investors make 21.7% (=0.1837/0.1509−1) more investments than the baseline firms. On the one hand, the influx of equity financing relaxes the financial constraints these firms face, enabling them to undertake profitable investments that they may have been unable to pursue otherwise. On the other hand, managers may view the sentiment-driven equity capital provided by meme stock investors as a heavily subsidized source of funding, which could lead them to invest in projects with low marginal returns.16 Consistent with this mechanism, the average MPK also declines from its baseline level of 0.1783 to 0.1636 among firms with the highest concentration of meme stock investors. Notably, this MPK is also slightly below the sum of the capital depreciation rate and the firms’ discount rate.
article.6. Investor demand and capital misalloca: Our previous results suggest that fluctuations in investor demand can have a substantial impact on firms’ optimal policies and investment returns. Naturally, one might wonder how these effects aggregate across firms. In this section, we explore this by showing that the impacts from investor demand do not simply wash out at the aggregate level; rather, they play a significant role in shaping the efficiency of capital allocation in the economy. We also compare the magnitude of these effects with other misallocation factors highlighted in prior research.6.1. Measuring capital misallocationUsing the equilibrium distribution of firms based on our model simulation, we next examine how the presence of investor excess demand affects the allocation of capital across firms. We focus on two measures: the variance of MPK and the TFP loss, both of which aim to capture the degree of capital allocation inefficiency for a cross section of firms in a given time period. We therefore drop the time subscript when describing these cross-sectional measures. We then calculate the time-series average of the measures to gauge the overall capital allocation efficiency for a model economy.Consider a model economy populated by heterogeneous firms with production function Yn=aζnznKnα. From the definition of MPK, we have: (22)Kn=MPKnαaζnzn1α−1.We sum Kn and Yn across firms to define the aggregate capital and output, K and Y, respectively. We then can calculate the aggregate TFP in our baseline model as: (23)TFP=YKα=∫naζnznMPKnαaζnznαα−1dn∫nMPKnαaζnzn1α−1dnα.In an efficient allocation without aggregate risk, capital can flow freely to its most productive user without any friction, maximizing static production. In this case, the MPK is equalized across firms. Denote the aggregate TFP in the efficient allocations as TFPe. We can define TFP loss as the difference between aggregate TFP in the efficient allocations and the baseline model: (24)TFPloss=logTFPe−logTFP.Hsieh and Klenow (2009) show that when zn and MPKn are jointly log-normally distributed, TFPloss will be linear in varlogMPKn, implying that TFP loss depends only on the dispersion of MPK and hence varlogMPKn is a sufficient statistic for TFP losses. However, when log-normality does not hold, the variance of MPK might not be a sufficient statistic for capital misallocation. Therefore, we employ both measures—the variance of MPK and the TFP loss—to gauge firms’ aggregate investment efficiency and the degree of misallocation.In Table 10, we present results indicating how investment efficiency measures change with investors’ excess demand. In panel A, we counterfactually vary excess demand by setting the mean (in logs) and standard deviation of excess demand shocks to 50% of the baseline value or zero, while holding all other parameters fixed at the estimated baseline values. Our results in rows (1) and (2) of Panel A suggest that excess demand plays a significant role in exacerbating capital misallocation across firms. When we counterfactually shut down investors’ excess demand, the dispersion of firms’ MPK becomes 26.9% less than that of the baseline model, and the TFP loss also declines by 23.36%. These findings indicate that investors’ excess demand imposes another friction on the efficient allocation of capital across firms. It provides firms with profit opportunities through financial transactions, which can create a conflicting incentive regarding how firms, especially the financially constrained ones, allocate their resources for investment efficiency.Table 10. In Panels B and C, we examine how the results differ if we also allow investors’ demand parameters β to vary. In particular, we consider cases where investors’ β is a convex combination of the estimated values in the baseline model and those of passive investors, whose demand depends only on market equity (i.e., βme=1) and not on other firm characteristics (βk=0 for k≠me). This exercise is particularly relevant given the increased presence of passive investors in the market. Our results in Panels B and C of Table 10 indicate that when investors’ β become more closely aligned with that of passive investors, excess demand triggers an even more substantial misallocation effect. In Panel C, for example, the dispersion of MPK and the TFP loss are 47.04% and 41.3% lower in the counterfactual economy without excess demand. Intuitively, holding all else constant, when βme is closer to one, any excess demand translates into a larger price impact (as shown in Eq. (13)), thus providing firms with greater opportunities to profit from the price deviation. As a result, the misallocation effect is amplified.Our results in Table 10 also offer a more nuanced view regarding how the increased presence of passive investors would influence the efficiency of capital allocation. Our analysis identifies two opposing effects of passive investing on capital allocation, which operate through β and ϵ. On the one hand, increased passive investing with inelastic demand (i.e., βme close to one) implies that given fluctuations in excess demand (ϵ) will translate into more significant deviations in equilibrium prices, which may take longer to converge, resulting in large dispersion in firms’ real investment returns. On the other hand, passive investors’ portfolio decisions are more likely to be shaped by firms’ fundamental characteristics, which leads to smaller variations in ϵ and reduced misallocation effects. Our results show that this latter effect is substantial.6.2. Comparison with other sources of misallocationHow significant are the effects of excess demand on capital misallocation compared with those of other financial and real frictions previously studied in the literature? To answer this question, we consider three such distortions: (1) the debt market frictions, (2) the real investment frictions, and (3) time-varying heterogeneous risk premia. To quantify the relative importance of each source, we perform three additional sets of counterfactual exercises by eliminating these frictions from our model one at a time. We then compare the resulting misallocation effects with those from investors’ excess demand, which we calculate in Section 6.1 and reproduce in Panel A of Table 11.In Panel B of Table 11, we conduct the first counterfactual exercise where we reduce debt market frictions by setting collateral constraint parameter θ to 3, allowing firms to borrow up to roughly their market value. The results provided in Panel B show that relaxing debt market frictions reduces the variance of MPK by 6.91% and the TFP losses by 2.74%.17 Midrigan and Xu (2014) and Moll (2014) find relatively limited impacts of collateral constraint on capital misallocation. Our model shares the same mechanism with those studies, wherein firms can save to alleviate the impact of collateral constraints. One unique feature of our model is that firms have heterogeneous capacities to accumulate savings due to their investors’ demand shocks. Quantitatively, the misallocation effects of relaxing collateral constraints are much smaller compared to those resulting from investors’ demand fluctuations. This is because the latter not only influences firms’ financial constraints but also directly provides them with subsidized equity capital. Additionally, firms are incentivized to adjust their investment and other policies to influence their characteristics, x, which directly affect investor demand and, thus, their ability to time the market in the current and future periods, leading to an amplified misallocation effect.Panel C of Table 11 presents the results of a counterfactual analysis in which we eliminate real investment frictions by setting the capital adjustment cost parameter to zero. Doing so reduces the variance of MPK by 3.04% and the TFP losses by 5.14%, both of which are smaller in magnitude compared to the effects of investors’ excess demand shocks. These findings are consistent with those of David and Venkateswaran (2019), highlighting that technological frictions, such as adjustment costs, account for only a limited portion of the observed misallocation.Panel D of Table 11 corresponds to a counterfactual exercise wherein we eliminate firms’ exposure to aggregate risk. According to David et al. (2022), firms’ heterogeneous exposure to time-varying aggregate risk is a key determinant of MPK, explaining around 25% of its dispersion. Our results in Panel D suggest that the variance of MPK and the TFP loss fall by 14.56% and 11.35%, respectively, when we eliminate firms’ exposure to aggregate risk. Quantitatively, these effects are smaller than those reported in David et al. (2022) since we explicitly model investors’ heterogeneous demand for firms’ equity based on their characteristics, including market beta. Therefore, the effect of heterogeneous risk exposure can be partially absorbed by the demand system. Based on the results in Panel D and those from David et al. (2022) as benchmarks, we conclude that the misallocation effect resulting from variations in investor demand is comparable to that from firms’ heterogeneous risk exposure.Overall, our findings indicate that fluctuations in investor demand can create significant barriers to efficient capital flows, leading to a sizeable misallocation effect.
article.7. Heterogeneous effects of excess deman: In this section, we examine the heterogeneous effects of excess demand. To motivate our analysis, we start by exploring the drivers underlying variations in investor demand, relating it to sentiment fluctuations not accounted for by the firm’s underlying fundamentals. Based on the results, we then perform subsample estimations to trace the extent to which the real impact of excess demand can vary across firms.7.1. Sources of excess demandTo shed light on the potential drivers of the variation in excess demand, we start by relating it to a multitude of well-established asset pricing factors. We consider 203 of these factors identified by Chen and Zimmermann (2022) and group them into two primary classifications: fundamental and non-fundamental. Fundamental factors are derived from firm-level accounting data, reflecting the company’s core business activities and financial well-being.18 This class encompasses signals such as accruals, asset growth, firm age, governance index, inventory growth, returns on assets, etc., totaling 102 distinct factors. Non-fundamental factors, on the other hand, do not stem directly from these intrinsic financial indicators. Instead, they tend to reflect human behavior and psychological tendencies that pervade the investment world. We identify non-fundamental factors as the universe of Chen and Zimmermann’s factors, excluding those we previously classified as fundamental. Our classification yields 101 non-fundamental factors.For the dependent variable in our regression, we construct model-implied ϵ by matching the model-predicted portfolio weight in Eq. (12) to the actual portfolio weight we observe for each stock in the data, backing out what ϵ the model calls for to match the data (as also discussed in Section 4.4). We compute the investor AUM-weighted excess demand for every firm quarter.Given the abundance of potential predictors and their substantial correlations, we use LASSO to address potential multicollinearity and overfitting and help with factor selection. After the LASSO shrinkage, we are left with 65 non-fundamental factors, which include signals such as analyst EPS forecast, maximum daily return over the month, option to stock volume, days with zero trades, short interest, etc. These non-fundamental factors collectively account for a significant 76.8% of the variation in our estimated ϵ. Further incorporating 86 fundamental factors after shrinkage only elevates the within-adjusted R-squared of the LASSO regression modestly to 79.8%. In Internet Appendix D.1, we further validate this result using the first 20 principal components of the fundamental and non-fundamental factors.After establishing the importance of non-fundamental factors, we next bring in more direct measures of investor sentiment to further demonstrate their relevance in driving excess demand. Our first measure is RavenPack’s Composite Sentiment Score (CSS) for news events, which calculates a score by averaging the CSS for all relevant news in each firm-quarter. Our second measure, Aggregate Event Volume (AEV), also from RavenPack, reflects the number of sentiment-based events per firm-quarter. Additionally, we include two more indicators: OIB, an investor sentiment measure based on retail investor buy-sell imbalances from the TAQ database following Kumar and Lee (2006), and ECC, a sentiment measure based on the tone of earnings conference calls as per Hassan et al. (2019). Our results in Internet Appendix D.1 show that all measures strongly predict excess demand, with statistical significance below 1%, jointly explaining more than 40% of cross-firm variation in model-implied excess demand.7.2. Subsample estimationOur previous findings imply that variations in investors’ excess demand are likely to stem from factors unrelated to firm fundamentals. They can mirror investor sentiment, as noted by Koijen and Yogo (2019). In this section, we trace the heterogeneous real impact of such variations in excess demand to study how the economic mechanism behind our model differs across subsamples of firms.To this end, we first split firms into subsamples based on their susceptibility to sentiment-driven investment behaviors. We consider two splits. The first is based on firm-level variations in the Composite Sentiment Score (CSS), which we refer to as “sentiment risk” (Dumas et al., 2009). Firms are classified as having high sentiment risk (“High SR”) if the time-series volatility of their CSS is above the sample median. The second split is based on the percentage of the shares held by household investors. Different types of investors tend to exhibit distinct patterns in sentiment-driven demand (Koijen and Yogo, 2019), and the household sector is arguably the most sensitive to sentiment fluctuation. Following this idea, we rank firms into deciles by the percentage of household holdings each quarter. Firms are assigned to the high household holdings (“High HH”) group if their average ranking is above the median.We re-estimate our model to match data moments calculated using each subsample. The purpose of our exercise is three-fold. First, it helps ascertain whether our estimation strategy can detect variations in demand from different types of investors, thus serving as informal “out-of-sample” validation tests of our model. Second, by comparing the properties of our parameter estimates across subsamples, we gain a better understanding of the sources of excess demand shocks. In particular, we confirm that excess demand can reflect investor sentiment by showing that fluctuation in estimated latent demand is indeed greater in subsamples dominated by investors whose portfolio choices are more likely to be sentiment-driven. Finally, while our baseline estimates allow us to gauge the aggregate impact of excess demand on firms’ returns on investment, the subsample analysis demonstrates the dispersion of such effects.The re-estimation results are reported in Table 12. Panel A confirms that firms with a higher fraction of household ownership and greater sentiment risk indeed experience significantly more volatile excess demand. The estimated σϵ for “High SR” firms is 13.2% (=0.1028/0.0908−1) larger than that for “Low SR” firms, with similar increases also seen between “Low HH” and “High HH” groups.Does more volatile investor demand lead to higher capital allocative inefficiency in these firms? The answer is ex-ante unclear. Panel B shows that the “High SR” and “High HH” firms also face different production technologies and financial frictions. Additionally, Panel C indicates they experience varying price elasticity in response to excess demand, meaning that a given level of excess demand causes smaller price increases for these firms. Moreover, these firms face a more negative price elasticity to equity issuance, meaning their share issuance creates greater price pressure, leading to a faster reversion of market prices to fundamental levels. These price impacts may limit high-sentiment firms’ ability to profit from financial transactions, dampening the effects of volatile investor demand.To account for the previously mentioned channels and assess their overall impact on firms’ investment efficiency, we repeat the analysis in Table 11 using estimates for each subsample. The results, shown in Panel D, indicate that variations in investors’ excess demand play a dominant role in shaping the efficiency of capital allocation. Columns (1) and (2) suggest that if all firms behave like those in the “High SR” subsample, eliminating investor excess demand would reduce the dispersion of MPK by 31.88% and TFP loss by 27.64%. These numbers capture the misallocative effect of investor demand among “High SR” firms, which is larger than that for the “Low SR” subsample. A similar pattern emerges when focusing on firms with varying household ownership shares.
article.8. Robustness and discussion: In this section, we further examine the robustness of our counterfactual experiment results to alternative estimation strategies and model specifications.First, we examine how the model-predicted misallocation effect changes when using demand parameters estimated through Koijen and Yogo’s IV approach, as reported in column (3) of Table 7. We conduct a counterfactual analysis similar to that in Panel A of Table 11, where we eliminate excess investor demand and assess the resulting impact on the variance of MPK and TPF loss in the economy. The effect may differ from our baseline for three reasons. First, their approach estimates more volatile excess demand; second, it generates a higher βme, which leads to lower price elasticity; and third, the loadings on other firm characteristics differ, implying a distinct feedback loop between investor demand and firm decisions. The result of this exercise is reported in column (2) of Table G.2 in the Internet Appendix, where the overall misallocation effect remains comparable to the baseline results. We also conduct a similar analysis using demand parameters estimated via the flow-based approach, and the results remain in line with our baseline findings, as shown in column (3) of Table G.2.Next, we explore an alternative model with a linear-quadratic and weakly convex equity issuance cost. When a firm issues new equity (E>0), we assume it incurs an equity issuance cost of Ψ(Et)=(ϕ0Kt+ϕ1Et+ϕ2Et2)×1{Et>0}. We reestimate this alternative model. As shown in Table G.5 in the Internet Appendix, the estimation yields a modest ϕ2 because our baseline model already endogenizes a negative price impact from equity issuance (Eq. (14)), which has similar effects as a convex issuance cost. Our results, reported in column (4) of Table G.2, suggest that investors’ excess demand triggers a similar capital misallocation effect in this alternative model compared with our baseline.We also consider non-convex capital adjustment costs, which take the form: (25)Ξ(It,Kt)≡Υ+Kt+ξ+It2Kt×1{It≥0}+Υ−Kt+ξ−It2Kt×1{It<0}.We choose Υ+=0.01 and Υ−=0.03, following Lin and Zhang (2013). We keep ξ+ at the baseline estimate of 0.3774 and set ξ−=1.2ξ+, so that adjusting capital downward is more costly. As shown in column (5) of Table G.2 in the Internet Appendix, we find that investors’ excess demand causes even greater misallocation in this alternative model. Due to the asymmetric adjustment costs, firms that experience negative shocks have to pay higher transaction costs to reduce their investments and finance equity market transactions. This leads to even more constrained investment decisions among these firms, further amplifying the distortive effects.Finally, we consider situations where a firm’s excess demand and its TFP shocks can be correlated. Specifically, we model: (26)lnzt+1lnϵt+1=ρzρzϵρϵzρϵ⋅lnztlnϵt+σz00σϵɛt+1,where the correlation coefficients ρϵz and ρzϵ can capture over- extrapolation of investors or reflect their superior private information, which can predict the firm’s future realizations of TFP. We calibrate these correlation coefficients by matching them to the lead–lag correlations between the model-implied ϵ and firms’ TFP constructed following Foster et al. (2008). We find both correlations are modest, with ρzϵ=0.041 and ρϵz=0.024. The extended model with correlated shocks reveals slightly smaller misallocation effects. As shown in column (6) in Table G.2 in the Internet Appendix, removing excess demand would reduce the firms’ MPK dispersion by 24.47% and the TPF loss by 20.80%.
